summary: Gruber critiques Apple's expansion of App Store advertising, particularly new ad placements on the Today tab and product pages that immediately filled with gambling, psychic, and hookup app ads. The rollout was so poorly managed that gambling ads appeared on pages for gambling addiction recovery apps, forcing Apple to pause certain ad categories within a day. Gruber argues the ad revenue cannot possibly justify the damage to Apple's brand and credibility, especially given Apple's longstanding rhetoric about not monetizing users. He contrasts Tim Cook's famous 'you're the product' criticism of ad-supported services with Apple now selling ads against users' attention in its own monopoly storefront. The piece also highlights how the mere presence of ads poisons perception of the entire App Store, with users now assuming even genuine editorial picks are paid placements.
key_points: Apple's new App Store ad placements were immediately dominated by gambling, psychic, and hookup ads, including on product pages for addiction recovery apps, forcing Apple to pause gambling-related ads within one day
The ad revenue cannot justify the reputational damage to Apple's brand, especially for a company sitting on $48.2 billion in cash

Phil Schiller's editorial redesign of the App Store was deliberately non-commercial, but now ads are undermining that trust — users already assumed editorial picks were paid placements before ads existed
Apple's advertising contradicts Tim Cook's repeated 'you're not the customer, you're the product' positioning against ad-supported business models
The presence of any ads, even clearly labeled ones, causes users to assume everything in the App Store is pay-for-play
money_quotes: No ads in the App Store, period would have been a powerful, appealing message. One that Apple could have used to justify its control over all software on the platform and its much-debated mandatory cut of all app and game transactions.

We sell ads in the App Store, but they're OK because they don't track you seems to be the message Apple is going for, but that's neither powerful nor appealing. It boils down to 'Hey, it could be worse.'
These App Store ads are like the 'Intel Inside' stickers on PC laptops — they're worth money, but the money's not worth it.
Apple's business model is no longer the straightforward selling of great products, and these new ads in the App Store are not designed to make anything better other than Apple's Services bottom line.
But how are these ads in the App Store not making users the product, and advertisers the customers?

If the message is that there are ads, but not many, but now there are more than there used to be — almost everyone is going to assume that anything that might be an ad is an ad and the whole App Store is pay-for-play all the way down.
The App Store has corrupted such a great company so deeply. They make so much from gambling and manipulative IAPs that they don't even see the problem anymore.
key_insight: Apple's App Store advertising undermines the company's most valuable competitive asset — its reputation for putting users first — for revenue that is trivial relative to its scale.
